data.title: Movado will restate certain financial statements due to misconduct at the Dubai branch of a subsidiary
data.description:
Movado Group (NYSE:MOV) disclosed on Friday that it became aware of allegations of misconduct within its Dubai branch of subsidiary, MGI Luxury Group Sárl that resulted in overstatement of sales, premature recognition of sales, and underreporting of credit notes owed to customers.

The actions of the former managing director and certain employees involved using an undisclosed third-party warehouse and falsifying documents to bypass internal controls, spanning approximately five years from fiscal year 2021. The investigation found no evidence of similar issues in other regions or involvement by employees outside the affected area. The managing director of the Dubai Branch has been terminated.

As a result of the incident, Movado determined that its consolidated financial statements for fiscal years 2022, 2023, and 2024, along with interim periods in fiscal years 2024 and 2025, require restatement to accurately reflect sales timing and credit issuance. The restated financials also include reduced operating expenses due to reversed accruals from adjusted operating results. Importantly, these misstatements did not affect the company's cash flows or compliance with debt covenants.

Movado (NYSE:MOV) said the investigation identified a material weakness in its internal controls over financial reporting. Specifically, the company’s risk assessment process failed to address risks tied to inadequate segregation of duties at the Dubai Branch. Management concluded that internal controls and disclosure procedures were ineffective during the affected periods. 

Movado (MOV) is scheduled to release earnings on April 16.

Shares of Movado Group (MOV) fell 4.9% in premarket trading on Friday, after shedding 4.7% on Thursday.

data.title: If China tariffs stick, iPhones could cost $2,000 or more, Wedbush warns
data.description:
The Trump administration has been adamant that it is about helping Main Street as opposed to Wall Street. If its tariff level of 145% on Chinese goods lasts for more than a few weeks, it's those on Main Street who could be feeling the pain the most, Wedbush Securities warned.

“Negotiations are key with China as well as if some tech companies get any forms of exemptions/carve outs from the tariffs such as Apple, Amazon, Nvidia, Tesla and others with the White House calling the shots,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients.

“Apple is in the eye of this storm and Cupertino has no choice but to price iPhones at $2k+ if these tariffs stick for more than a few weeks,” the analysts at the firm added.

China “holds a lot of cards” in the proverbial economic poker game, the analysts added, with heavy influence over the economy, the U.S. Treasury market. It also has considerable sway over Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), even as the tech giant has worked to diversify its supply chain in recent years, moving manufacturing to countries like Vietnam, India, Brazil and elsewhere.

Nonetheless, it's estimated that at least 50% of iPhone production comes from China, along with more than 50% of Macs and 75% to 80% of iPads.

data.title: European markets rebound after China hits back, Euro at 3-year high
data.description:
Editor's note: The revised post reflects market movements as of 7.15am ET.

London (UKX) +0.85% to 7,980. UK GDP rises 1.4% Y/Y in February.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.62% to 20,433.

France (CAC:IND) +0.03% to 7,129.

Spain's inflation drops to 2.3% in March.

The Czech Republic’s current account surplus widened to CZK 43.79B in February.

The British pound climbed past $1.30, moving closer to a six-month high of $1.31.

Industrial production in Hungary shrank by 8.7% Y/Y in February. Industrial production in Hungary shrank by 8.7% Y/Y in February.

Slovakia’s construction output rose by 3.4% Y/Y in February.

Switzerland’s consumer confidence came at -35 in March.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) edged 0.14% higher to 487.77, after a rally the previous day. The index lost almost 2.2% this week. ECB officials cautioned that U.S. tariffs and potential retaliatory measures could drive inflation higher, at least in the near term.

The euro (EUR:USD) traded 0.84% higher on Friday to 1.1353, touching the highest mark in three years at 1.1387 as investors flee U.S. assets due to ongoing tariff uncertainties.  

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up less than 1 basis point to 4.40%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 5 basis points to 2.54%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 4 basis points to 4.69%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK).

data.title: Argenx, Halozyme win FDA nod for Vyvgart Hytrulo prefilled syringe
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved Dutch biotech argenx’s (NASDAQ:ARGX) (OTCPK:ARGNF) autoimmune disease drug Vyvgart Hytrulo, developed with Halozyme Therapeutics (NASDAQ:HALO), as a prefilled syringe for self-injection.

The treatment was approved for adults suffering from two autoimmune disorders: those with generalized myasthenia gravis (who are positive for anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies) and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP).

The antibody drug will therefore be indicated as a 20-to-30-second subcutaneous injection to be administered by a patient, caregiver, or healthcare professional, the companies said.

The approval adds the third administration option for Vyvgart Hytrulo, which was first approved by the FDA in June 2023 for gMG and later expanded to cover CIDP in June 2024.

“Today’s FDA approval provides a new self-injection option across both approved indications in the U.S. that is designed for patients who seek more independence with their treatment,” noted ARGX’s medical chief Luc Truyen.
